input.prompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

input.initialPrompt:
I'm building a chat messaging screen in .NET MAUI targeting .NET 10. When the
keyboard opens, it covers my text input at the bottom of the screen. In .NET 9
I used WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize but that doesn't seem to work anymore.
How do I make the input bar move up when the keyboard appears?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a .NET MAUI issue where the keyboard covers the text input at the bottom of the screen. They mention that `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` worked in .NET 9 but doesn't work in .NET 10. Let me provide guidance on how to handle this in .NET 10.

In .NET MAUI for .NET 9, the Android soft input mode was controlled via `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize`, but this was deprecated in Android API 30+ (Android 11+). The recommended approach changed.

For .NET 10 / .NET MAUI, the approach depends on the platform:


- On Android, `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` is no longer reliable due to Android 11+ deprecations. The modern solution involves using `WindowInsets` with edge-to-edge display and `WindowCompat.SetDecorFitsSystemWindows(window, false)` along with inset listeners to handle keyboard adjustments properly.

- In .NET MAUI 10, the platform-specific API `App.Current.On<Android>().UseWindowSoftInputModeAdjust(WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize)` may still exist but isn't the recommended approach. The framework now favors `KeyboardAutoManagerScroll`, which handles keyboard scrolling automatically in most scenarios, though the deprecated `Resize` mode might still function as a fallback if needed.

- For chat UIs specifically, wrapping content in a `ScrollView` combined with the automatic keyboard scrolling should handle the layout adjustments properly, though I should verify whether there are any edge cases or configuration nuances I'm missing here.

The issue is that `WindowSoftInputModeAdjust.Resize` no longer works in .NET 10 because of the edge-to-edge display becoming the default—the app now draws behind system bars and keyboard insets need different handling. I need to either manage `WindowInsets` for the IME to add padding to bottom content, or disable edge-to-edge entirely to fall back on the older soft input mode approach.
